---
title: "Calibration Analysis"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
execute:
  warning: false
---

# Goal

Inspect how well the model's predicted probabilities match observed funding
rates, then compare two post-hoc calibrators (Platt scaling and isotonic
regression) on the held-out folds produced by the walk-forward runner.

# Inputs

This notebook expects the runner output (`scores` frame) saved to disk by an
upstream evaluation run. To regenerate, run:

```python
from evaluation.runner import run_evaluation
out = run_evaluation(cutoffs, spec, funding_events)
out["scores"].to_parquet("eval_scores.parquet")
```

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.isotonic import IsotonicRegression
from sklearn.metrics import brier_score_loss
```

# Load scored data

```{python}
#| eval: false
SCORES_PATH = "eval_scores.parquet"
scores = pd.read_parquet(SCORES_PATH)
xgb = scores[scores["model"] == "xgboost"].copy()
xgb.head()
```

# Reliability diagram (raw model)

Decile bins of predicted probability vs observed positive rate.

```{python}
#| eval: false
def reliability_curve(probs, labels, n_bins=10):
    # Use quantile-based bins so each decile has a similar count.
    edges = np.quantile(probs, np.linspace(0, 1, n_bins + 1))
    edges[0] -= 1e-9  # include the smallest value
    bins = np.digitize(probs, edges) - 1
    bins = np.clip(bins, 0, n_bins - 1)
    df = pd.DataFrame({"bin": bins, "p": probs, "y": labels})
    return df.groupby("bin").agg(predicted=("p", "mean"), observed=("y", "mean"), n=("y", "size"))

curve = reliability_curve(xgb["score"].to_numpy(), xgb["label"].to_numpy())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "--", color="gray", label="perfect")
ax.plot(curve["predicted"], curve["observed"], "o-", label="xgboost (raw)")
ax.set_xlabel("Mean predicted probability (decile)")
ax.set_ylabel("Observed positive rate")
ax.legend(); ax.set_title("Reliability diagram")
plt.show()
```

# Platt scaling vs isotonic regression

Fit each calibrator on the first half of the folds and evaluate on the
second half (so the calibrator never sees the data it scores).

```{python}
#| eval: false
xgb = xgb.sort_values("as_of_date")
cutoffs = sorted(xgb["as_of_date"].unique())
split = cutoffs[len(cutoffs) // 2]

fit = xgb[xgb["as_of_date"] < split]
test = xgb[xgb["as_of_date"] >= split]

platt = LogisticRegression()
platt.fit(fit[["score"]].to_numpy(), fit["label"].to_numpy())
test = test.assign(p_platt=platt.predict_proba(test[["score"]].to_numpy())[:, 1])

iso = IsotonicRegression(out_of_bounds="clip")
iso.fit(fit["score"].to_numpy(), fit["label"].to_numpy())
test = test.assign(p_iso=iso.predict(test["score"].to_numpy()))

results = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "method": ["raw", "platt", "isotonic"],
        "brier": [
            brier_score_loss(test["label"], test["score"]),
            brier_score_loss(test["label"], test["p_platt"]),
            brier_score_loss(test["label"], test["p_iso"]),
        ],
    }
)
results
```

# Side-by-side reliability

```{python}
#| eval: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 7))
for label, col in [("raw", "score"), ("platt", "p_platt"), ("isotonic", "p_iso")]:
    c = reliability_curve(test[col].to_numpy(), test["label"].to_numpy())
    ax.plot(c["predicted"], c["observed"], "o-", label=label)
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "--", color="gray")
ax.set_xlabel("Predicted probability")
ax.set_ylabel("Observed rate")
ax.legend(); ax.set_title("Calibration comparison (held-out folds)")
plt.show()
```

# Takeaways

- Brier score: lower is better; expect isotonic to win on enough data,
  Platt to be more stable on small folds.
- If the raw curve is monotonic but biased, Platt is fine; if it's wavy,
  isotonic is better.
- Decision: pick the calibrator whose top-decile observed rate is
  closest to its predicted rate, since that's the slice we use for
  outreach ranking.
